coredata.dc:title: A novel and highly stable dual-emission carbon dots-based phosphor
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Mengmeng
Liu, Yunpeng
Zhu, Mengmeng
Xia, Jinfeng
Xuan, Tongtong
Jiang, Danyu
Zhou, Guohong
Li, Huili
coredata.dc:description: A novel green/red dual-emission carbon dots-based phosphor (named as GRCDs phosphor) was successfully prepared by introducing calcium hydroxide [Ca(OH)2] as matrix material mixed with green-emitting carbon dots (CDs) for the first time. The addition of Ca(OH)2 not only prevents fluorescent self-quenching caused by polymerization of CDs in the solid state but also promotes the red emission of CDs due to abundant functional groups on the surface. Furthermore, the detailed characterizations of the structure and optical properties show that the hydroxyl groups play a crucial role for the red emission of GRCDs phosphor. As-prepared GRCDs phosphor possesses outstanding thermal stability as well as excellent water stability. This finding provides a new way for the development of CDs-based phosphors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Phosphors
Photoluminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis process of GRCDs phosphors (a), the absorption spectra of CDs and GRCDs phosphors (b) and PL spectra of CDs, Ca(OH)2 and GRCDs phosphors under excitation of 420 nm (c).
The TEM and HRTEM (inset) of CDs (a), HRTEM and selected area electron diffraction (SAED, inset) of GRCDs phosphors (b), XRD patterns (c) and Raman spectra (d) of CDs and GRCDs phosphors.
The XPS spectra of CDs and GRCDs phosphors (a) as well with FTIR spectra of CDs, Ca(OH)2 and GRCDs phosphors (b).
The possible PL mechanism of GRCDs phosphors.

The photographs of GRCDs phosphors stored in air for 0 (a) and 3 months (b) under the irradiation of 365 nm, and the corresponding XRD patterns (c), TGA curves of CDs (d), and GRCDs phosphors (e) from room temperature to 800 °C, as well as the normalized integrated intensity from 480 nm to 730 nm for GRCDs phosphors heated to various temperatures (25–275 °C) in air atmosphere (f).

head.ce:title: A novel and highly stable dual-emission carbon dots-based phosphor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, Fujian, China.
Correspondence to: East China Normal University, Shanghai 200241, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel green/red dual-emission carbon dots-based phosphor (named as GRCDs phosphor) was successfully prepared by introducing calcium hydroxide [Ca(OH)2] as matrix material mixed with green-emitting carbon dots (CDs) for the first time. The addition of Ca(OH)2 not only prevents fluorescent self-quenching caused by polymerization of CDs in the solid state but also promotes the red emission of CDs due to abundant functional groups on the surface. Furthermore, the detailed characterizations of the structure and optical properties show that the hydroxyl groups play a crucial role for the red emission of GRCDs phosphor. As-prepared GRCDs phosphor possesses outstanding thermal stability as well as excellent water stability. This finding provides a new way for the development of CDs-based phosphors.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as one of the potential luminescent carbon nanomaterials, have attracted widespread attention in academics and set off a research upsurge all over the world since they were first discovered by Walter et al. in 2004 [1]. CDs are quasi-spherical nanocrystals with size typically less than 10 nm, consisting of internal carbon core and surface functional groups and defects. CDs possess the advantages of simple preparation methods, cheap and available raw materials [2–5], excellent fluorescence properties, good biocompatibility, excellent photostability, environmental friendliness and low toxicity [6,7]. Therefore, they are widely used in bioimaging [8–10], pH detection [11,12], catalysis [13,14], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [15–17], and so on. However, CDs usually suffer from the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching (AILQ) in solid state which limits their applications in optoelectronic devices [18]. Recently, many efforts have been proposed to settle the AILQ problem of CDs in solid state, for example, KH-792 [19,20], polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [21], starch [22,23], sodium silicate [24], silica [25], sodium chloride (NaCl) [26], silica gel [27], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [28] etc. were selected as matrixes, which can effectively control the distances among CDs to avoid AILQ caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and/or direct π-π interaction.

So far, most of the reported CDs-based phosphors usually give single-peak emission, which cannot meet the requirements of multi-color emission materials in many applications. For example, in temperature detection application, the fluorescence intensity of sensor is affected by the change of oxygen and pH value besides temperature. However, the dual-emission CDs based phosphor can be designed as a fluorescent sensor ratiometer to effectively avoid these problems [29]. Additionally, the dual-emission spectrum generally covers a wide wavelength range, which is beneficial to the fabrication of white LEDs (WLEDs). Therefore, CDs with multicolor emissions under solid-state condition are still very ideal materials in various applications, but there are few reports on CDs-based phosphors with dual fluorescence emission. Lei et al. reported a double emitting CDs-based fluorescent material by dispersing blue- and red-emission CDs on the surface and in the pores of mesoporous alumina matrix, which showed excellent color stability. White emission could be easily obtained only by adjusting the excitation wavelength because of the dual emission property of used CDs-based phosphors. Finally, a WLED excited by ultraviolet chip was fabricated [30]. But the composite consisted of two kinds of CDs maybe own the defects of self-absorption and different stabilities which are not conducive to the applications. The luminous efficiency and stability of LEDs are usually decreased. If the dual-emission solid-state CDs can be prepared by one step without doping two or more kinds of CDs, it is undoubtedly to promote the further development and applications of CDs in related fields.

Alkali has been reported to play a pivotal role in the surface electron state of CDs. The introduction of alkali enriches the surface electrons of CDs, and raises the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to a higher energy level due to the strong interaction between electrons and conjugated system and decreases the band gap, thereby promotes the redshift of CDs fluorescence emission [31]. Calcium hydroxide is an intermediate product in the production of light calcium with the formula Ca(OH)2 and has the advantages of low cost, antimicrobial, non-toxic, stable chemical properties and good biocompatibility. It plays an important role in food, medicine and environmental protection, etc [32–34]. It is classified as strong base with high pH because Ca(OH)2 could dissociate into Ca2+ and OH- ions on contact with aqueous fluids and it has low solubility in water about 1.2 g/L (25 °C) [35,36]. The strong alkalinity of Ca(OH)2 provides an advantageous condition for the lower energy emission of CDs. And the sparingly water-soluble property makes the CDs-based phosphors keep outstanding stability in water when Ca(OH)2 is used as a matrix. As far as we know, there are no related reports on the preparation of CDs-based phosphors using Ca(OH)2 as matrix material. This work may promote the further development of CDs and their related applications.

Herein, we synthesized green fluorescent CDs using citric acid and urea as reaction materials by one-step solvothermal method. Then, the dual emission CDs-based phosphors covering green and red region were gained by mixing CDs and Ca(OH)2 with a certain ratio for the first time. CDs are chemically adsorbed on the surface of Ca(OH)2 through the interaction of surface functional groups, which can increase the distance among CDs to overcome AILQ. As-prepared GRCDs phosphors not only possess excellent optical properties but also have promising thermal and water stability. These outstanding properties make the prepared CDs-based phosphors own intensely potential applications in the general lighting devices and other related fields.

For all experiments, citric acid (CA), urea, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and calcium hydroxide [Ca(OH)2] were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals are analytical reagents and used as received without any refining treatment.

Citric acid and urea were mixed at a molar ratio of 0.156:1 in DMF to form a transparent solution. The clarified solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 6 h. After this, the deep red suspension was obtained, then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min and washed using deionized water for three times. The precipitate was collected and freeze-dried to gain dark products. The CDs solution was obtained through re-dissolving dark products in deionized water for further characterization and analysis.

1.5 g Ca(OH)2 was added to 20 ml deionized water to form a suspension solution. 0.1 ml of the above-mentioned CDs solution was added to the suspension solution and stirred for 20 min. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and then the precipitation was collected. The solid-state CDs powders were obtained by drying the precipitation at 60 °C for 4 h under vacuum, named as GRCDs-phosphors. As the comparative experiments, calcium carbonate (CaCO3) was selected as matrix to prepare phosphors according to the same step above, recorded as CDs1-phosphors. Excessive HCl was added to as-prepared GRCDs phosphors to form CDs-CaCl2 composites, named as CDs2-phosphors.

Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, FluoroMax-4). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded by Hitachi U-3900. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using a D2 X-ray diffractometer (Bruker). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, NEXUS 670) were used for the element compositions and functional groups analysis of samples. Raman spectra were collected by using Raman Spectrometer (RM-1000, Renishaw). The morphology and fluorescence image of products were characterized by JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) and fluorescence microscope. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was recorded using a thermogravimetric analyzer (LD12-HIR) with the temperature from 30 to 800 °C under inert atmosphere. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were carried out in the electrolyte (0.1 M) prepared using anhydrous acetonitrile and tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate. The reference electrode was Ag/Ag+ couple. The powders were placed in the sample chamber and then heated to the desired temperature by a high-temperature fluorescence controller (Tianjin Orient KOJI Co., LTD., TAP-02) to measure the thermal stability of phosphors.

As shown in Fig. 1a, we first synthesized CDs solution in DMF using CA and urea through a solvothermal method, and then dual-emission GRCDs-phosphors could be obtained by mixing the CDs solution and Ca(OH)2 together. In this case, CA was used as the precursor of carbon source, while N doping source arose from urea. The absorption spectra of CDs in aqueous solution and as-prepared GRCDs-phosphors are given in Fig. 1b. The peaks at 225 nm and 331 nm are typical π-π* transition of C═C and n-π* transition of C═O, respectively. The broad absorption peaks from 400 to 600 nm may originate from the n-π* transition of π systems containing C─N/C═N or C─O structure [37–40]. Conversely, unlike the CDs solution, the absorption peak at 339 nm of GRCDs-phosphors is decreased with the enhancement of the absorption band in the visible region at 400–600 nm and the absorption band edge redshifts from 700 nm to near-infrared about 800 nm. Fig. 1c shows the PL spectra of the CDs, Ca(OH)2 and GRCDs-phosphors. It can be seen that there is one characteristic emission peak for CDs at 532 nm under the excitation of 420 nm. Interestingly, as-prepared GRCDs-phosphors exhibit double emission peaks at 525 nm and 602 nm under the same excitation wavelength. Undoubtedly, emission at 602 nm should be attributed to the introducing of Ca(OH)2. As we know that Ca(OH)2 has no fluorescent emission (Fig. 1c). The solid-state CDs obtained by combing with Ca(OH)2 still possess positive fluorescence perhaps owing to the increased distance between particles after adding Ca(OH)2, which effectively hinders the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), thus overcomes the self-fluorescence quenching barrier caused by polymerization [19,41]. While the green and red dual-emission of GRCDs-phosphors may be due to the change of surface functional groups.

To understand the PL mechanism, the morphology, structure and surface groups of the CDs and GRCDs-phosphors were investigated systematically. The morphology of CDs was characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 2a. As-prepared CDs exhibit a spherical shape and uniform size distribution. The average particle size is 3.25 nm (Fig. S1). No obvious crystalline structure could be observed from the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), suggesting the poor crystallinity of CDs (inset of Fig. 2a). Only Ca(OH)2 particles can be seen for pure Ca(OH)2 powders in Fig. S2. Distinct lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.15 nm corresponds to the (201) plane of Ca(OH)2. In contrast, the CDs spherical particles clearly exist on the Ca(OH)2 particles, as seen from HRTEM image of the GRCDs phosphors (Fig. 2b). It means that CDs are embedded in Ca(OH)2 and the effective combination between them. Both SAED patterns in the insets of Fig. S2 and Fig. 2b show polycrystalline Ca(OH)2, illustrating that the addition of CDs does not change the crystal properties of host material.

The phase structures of CDs and GRCDs phosphors were further characterized by XRD and are shown in Fig. 2c. A broad diffraction peak centered at around 26.7° is assigned to the graphene-like structure with (002) planes for CDs, which is covered by the excessive Ca(OH)2 in the GRCDs phosphors. And as a result, the XRD pattern of GRCDs phosphors only shows the Ca(OH)2 phase with main diffraction peaks located at 18.01°, 28.67°, 34.10°, 47.12°, 50.81°, 54.36°, 62.63°, 64.23° and 71.81° corresponding to (001), (100), (101), (102), (110), (111), (201), (103) and (202) planes, respectively (PDF#44-1481) [42]. In addition, the Raman spectrum of CDs contains two characteristic peaks at 1354 cm−1 and 1583 cm−1 resulting from the disordered D band and the graphitic G band, respectively (Fig. 2d). The co-existence of D band and G band indicates that the prepared CDs have a partially disordered carbon structure [43]. The characteristic Raman peaks of CDs are also covered by the excessive Ca(OH)2 in the GRCDs phosphors. These results match well with XRD in Fig. 2c.

To understand the PL properties of the GRCDs phosphors, the element compositions and surface functional groups of CDs and GRCDs phosphors were carefully analyzed by the XPS and FTIR spectra in Fig. 3. Three peaks located at 285.2 eV, 399.5 eV and 531.2 eV are assigned to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 3a), indicating that the prepared CDs are composed of C, N and O elements. The deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra in Fig. S3 confirm the existence of C═C (284.6 eV), C-N (285.3 eV), C-O (286.7 eV), and C═O (288.1 eV) for C 1s; C═O (531.0 eV) and C-O (531.8 eV) for O 1s; pyridinic N (399.0 eV), pyrrolic N (400.0 eV), and graphitic N (401.5 eV) for N 1s [44]. However, besides the signal peaks of C, N, and O, Ca 2p and Ca 2s peaks can also be observed in the GRCDs phosphors, indicating that CDs are effectively combined with Ca(OH)2, which is in good agreement with the results of TEM. Compared with pure CDs, the C and N contents of GRCDs phosphors decrease and O content increases significantly due to the addition of a large amount of Ca(OH)2 matrix, as shown in Table S1. Correspondingly, the signal peaks of oxygen-containing groups in high-resolution XPS spectra of GRCDs phosphors (Fig. S4) obviously enhance because of the massive -OH groups introduced by Ca(OH)2, which effectively combine with surface functional groups of CDs through chemical bonds.

According to the above discussion, it is speculated that the surface functional groups of CDs embedded in the Ca(OH)2 matrix have been changed greatly, and as a result, PL properties of the GRCDs phosphors were directly adjusted. For further proving, FTIR spectra of CDs, Ca(OH)2, and GRCDs phosphors were investigated and given in Fig. 3b. The existence of functional groups in CDs, such as O-H and N-H at 3434 cm−1, C═C and C═O at 1669–1613 cm−1, reveals the excellent hydrophilicity of CDs. For GRCDs phosphors, the characteristic absorption peak at 875 cm−1 is attributed to Ca-O-H [45], corresponding well to FTIR characteristic peak of pure Ca(OH)2. The stronger absorption peak at 3434 cm−1 for GRCDs phosphors than pure Ca(OH)2 indicates the effective combination of CDs with Ca(OH)2 surface groups. Simultaneously, the absorption band strength at 1669–1613 cm−1 is significantly weaker than that of CDs, which is similar to the XPS analysis due to the decrease of C and N contents (Fig. 3a). The most striking difference is the appearance of absorption peak at 1453 cm−1 belonging to the in-plane bending vibration of -OH in GRCDs phosphors [46], which also exists in pure Ca(OH)2 powders but is absence in the CDs. Thus, we can safely conclude that the abundant -OH in GRCDs phosphors results from the introduction of Ca(OH)2. In other words, the red emission band of GRCDs phosphors is mostly due to the addition of a large number of -OH on the CDs surface.

To verify the crucial role of -OH in GRCDs phosphors, CaCO3 and CDs were used to obtain phosphors in the same way, denoted as CDs1-phosphors. The photograph of CDs1-phosphors under 365 nm UV light illumination (Fig. S5) shows no fluorescence emission. Additionally, excessive HCl was added to as-prepared GRCDs phosphors to make Ca(OH)2 react completely to form CaCl2, synthesizing CDs-CaCl2 composites, named as CDs2-phosphors. The XRD result shows that the Ca(OH)2 completely transforms into CaCl2 after the addition of HCl, and the existence of CDs has no effect on the CaCl2 phase structure (Fig. S6a). As the -OH is destroyed, the red emission band of powders thoroughly disappears, and the CDs2-phosphors exhibit green emission centered at 520 nm (Fig. S6b) which even blueshifts 12 nm compared with the emission at 532 nm of pure CDs. It should be noted that CaCl2 does not emit any fluorescence under UV light irradiation (Fig. S6c). Based on these results, we are more certain that the red emission is associated with the increased -OH on the surface of the CDs for GRCDs phosphors, which had been proven by Wu and co-workers [38]. Qu et al. have also reported that sodium hydroxide/potassium hydroxide is beneficial to the output of red emission of CDs [37]. However, due to their high solubility, a high additive concentration is required to form solid, which is extremely dangerous and is not conducive to the preparation of solid CDs with high stability.

At present, there is no clear conclusion on PL mechanism of CDs. Many reports declared that the PL of CDs was dependent on size [47–49], conjugated structure [50,51], molecule state [52,53], surface state [54–56], etc. Among these proposed PL mechanisms, surface state-related PL emission has attracted much attention. Zhang and co-workers revealed that the energy gap of CDs was governed by both the size and degree of surface oxidation [57]. Chen and co-workers’ theoretical calculation [58] indicated that the emission wavelength of oxidized graphene quantum dots (GQD) strongly depended on the coverage of -OH and/or -COOH groups. Attaching both -OH and -COOH groups to the GQD continuously changed GQD emission from green (572.4 nm) to red (732.3 nm for -OH and 612.3 nm for -COOH). In our work, Ca(OH)2 introduces a large number of -OH in GRCDs phosphors as supported by FTIR spectra, which may lead to the change of electronic states on the surface of CDs. Because -OH is a strong electron-donating group which can increase electron cloud density of the conjugated system. In order to better illustrate the above viewpoint and understand PL mechanism with the action of -OH, cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of CDs and GRCDs phosphors were measured and given in Fig. S7. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) can be calculated by the following equations [59]:
(1)
E
HOMO
= −
E
ox
+ 4.80
eV
(2)
E
LUMO
= -
E
HOMO
−
E g
eV
where
E
ox
is onset oxidation potential and
E
g
is energy gap which can be obtained by Tauc plots [60]:
(3)
α hv
2
= A
hv −
E
g

h is the Planck constant, v is the light frequency, A is a physical quantity related to materials, α is the absorbance. Taking
α hv
2
as y-axis and hv as x-axis, the linear section is fitted to obtain a linear equation, and the x-intercept is the bandgap of material. Tauc plots for the bandgaps of CDs and GRCDs phosphors were measured in Fig. S8. The HOMO of CDs is obtained as −5.44 eV, the related energy gap of the CDs is calculated as 3.58 eV. Then the LUMO of CDs is gained as −1.86 eV. The HOMO, LUMO, and bandgap of GRCDs phosphors are calculated by the same method as −5.39 eV, −2.31 eV, 3.08 eV, respectively. These data indicate that the addition of -OH on the surface of the CDs leads to a narrower bandgap, which is the reason for the red emission. According to these, a possible mechanism is suggested in Fig. 4. Green-emitting CDs were combined with Ca(OH)2 to prepare green-red double-emission CDs phosphors. After the material is excited, the electrons jump from the ground state to the excited state and then return to the ground state through the radiative transition, resulting in green fluorescence. When the -OH is introduced, the bandgap of CDs becomes smaller, a part of the excited electrons jump to a lower electronic energy level, which is responsible for the output of long-wavelength fluorescence. Finally, green and red dual-emission is achieved in GRCDs phosphors.

Ca(OH)2 can easily absorb CO2 in air and react with it to produce calcium carbonate (CaCO3). In comparative experiment, the synthesized CDs1-phosphors by using CaCO3 as matrix material could not emit fluorescence (Fig. S5). However, the as-prepared GRCDs phosphors under the excitation of 365 nm still display obvious fluorescence emission and stable phase structure after storing in air for three months ( Fig. 5a, b, and c), showing the excellent stability of GRCDs phosphors. Furthermore, GRCDs phosphors were redispersed by ultrasound and completely exposed to the deionized water for 30 min and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 2 h. The GRCDs phosphors treated in deionized water still retain the original shape and bright emission (Fig. S9). It illustrates that as-prepared GRCDs phosphors have superior air and water stability. As we know, the excellent thermal stability is very important to phosphors for potential applications especially in optoelectronic devices [61,62]. Therefore, TGA was carried out and shown in Fig. 5d and e for CDs and GRCDs phosphors, respectively. The CDs show a weight loss of 17% at 200 °C that is stemming from the evaporation of water molecules bonded to CDs [63]. However, the GRCDs phosphors only give a weight loss of 1.2% even the temperature arises to 360 °C (Fig. 5e). The main weightlessness stage locates at 380–450 °C, which is attributed to the decomposition of Ca(OH)2 [45]. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of GRCDs phosphors at 270 °C still maintains 87% of the initial intensity (Fig. 5f), which is even better than the stable Ba2Si5N8 phosphors whose emission intensity at 150 °C decreases to 77% of that at room temperature [64], indicating that as-prepared GRCDs phosphors own perfect thermal stability. All these outstanding properties make the GRCDs phosphors can be used as potential luminescent materials for general lighting devices and other broader fields.

In summary, we first synthesized the green and red dual-emission CDs phosphors by combing Ca(OH)2 and green-emitting CDs. The addition of Ca(OH)2 introduces a large number of -OH groups with strong electron-donating property, which leads to an increase of the electron cloud density in the conjugate system, lifts HOMO to a higher energy level, and as a result, decreases the band gap. When the GRCDs phosphors are excited, a part of the excited state electrons jump to a lower energy level to produce red-emitting fluorescence. Finally, GRCDs phosphors emitting green and red dual colors are achieved successfully. Detailed characterizations at high temperature and in the high humidity prove that the as-prepared GRCDs phosphors possess the excellent thermal and water stability, which is a great advance for CDs-based solid-state phosphors, and can promote their applications in the field of optoelectronic devices.

Mengmeng Cao: Preparing CDs and GRCDs phosphors, testing and writing. Yunpeng Liu: Assisting PL, UV-Vis absorption spectra test. Mengmeng Zhu: Assisting XRD test. Danyu Jiang, Guohong Zhou: Reviewing and editing. Jinfeng Xia*, Tongtong Xuan*, Huili Li*: Writing, reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Shanghai Municipal Natural Science Foundation (No. 19ZR1415400) and East China Normal University-University of Alberta Joint Institute of Advanced Science and Technology (JIAST) (No. 40500-20105-222053), and the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (No. 2020J01035).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile, green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from oatmeal for cell imaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress on the photocatalysis of carbon dots: classification, mechanism and applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with red/near-infrared emissions and their intrinsic merits for biomedical applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel properties and applications of carbon nanodots
